skill.name: deploying-postgres-k8s
skill.description: Guides deployment of PostgreSQL on Kubernetes with CloudNativePG, including operator installation, cluster manifests, credentials, backups, monitoring, and cleanup.
skill.summary: Deploy PostgreSQL clusters on Kubernetes with CloudNativePG high availability patterns.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many command-execution, network, credential, and weak-crypto alerts. Manual review found no malicious intent, prompt injection, obfuscation, or credential exfiltration; most alerts are Kubernetes documentation examples or false positives. The skill remains medium risk because users may apply remote manifests, reveal database secrets, or run destructive cluster commands.

medium_findings.title: Remote Kubernetes Manifests Applied From URLs
medium_findings.description: The instructions apply and delete CloudNativePG manifests directly from hardcoded external URLs. This is legitimate for operator installation, but users should verify upstream manifests and versions before applying them to a cluster.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL-based kubectl commands are explicit in the Markdown and would change cluster state if copied. The context is legitimate CloudNativePG installation, so this is an operational supply-chain warning rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Database Secrets Displayed Or Embedded In Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill shows commands that decode Kubernetes database connection secrets and includes placeholder DATABASE_URL strings with passwords. These are normal database administration examples, but they can leak credentials through terminals, shell history, or copied manifests if used unchanged.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly show decoded secret retrieval and password-bearing connection strings. The values appear to be examples, so the risk is exposure through operator misuse, not confirmed exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Privileged Cluster Commands In Documentation
low_findings.description: The static command-execution findings are Markdown examples for kubectl, helm, psql, and alembic, not code executed automatically by the skill. They still require care because they create, modify, or delete Kubernetes and database resources.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present in fenced Markdown blocks and are consistent with this DevOps skill. They are not hidden execution paths, but they can affect production infrastructure when run.

low_findings.title: Cleanup Example Includes Data-Deleting PVC Command
low_findings.description: The cleanup section includes a command that deletes persistent volume claims for the PostgreSQL cluster. The file labels this as data loss, so the issue is documented but remains important for users.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command and the data-loss warning are both visible in the same cleanup block. This is a clear operational hazard, not malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static Critical Heuristic Dismissed After Context Review
low_findings.description: The analyzer combined command examples, external URLs, and database configuration examples into a dangerous-combination alert. Review found instructional Markdown and a local verifier only; no prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or automatic network execution was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context shows ordinary Kubernetes documentation and a verifier that only reads SKILL.md. Confidence is high, although users must still review commands before applying them to real clusters.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-crypto locations do not use weak cryptographic algorithms. The only related command generates a random password with openssl rand, which is appropriate for example secret creation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no DES, MD5, SHA1, or similar weak algorithm use at the cited locations. The openssl rand command creates random bytes rather than hashing or encrypting data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Kubernetes Manifests Applied From URLs
dangerous_patterns.description: The instructions apply and delete CloudNativePG manifests directly from hardcoded external URLs. This is legitimate for operator installation, but users should verify upstream manifests and versions before applying them to a cluster.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL-based kubectl commands are explicit in the Markdown and would change cluster state if copied. The context is legitimate CloudNativePG installation, so this is an operational supply-chain warning rather than malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Database Secrets Displayed Or Embedded In Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows commands that decode Kubernetes database connection secrets and includes placeholder DATABASE_URL strings with passwords. These are normal database administration examples, but they can leak credentials through terminals, shell history, or copied manifests if used unchanged.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly show decoded secret retrieval and password-bearing connection strings. The values appear to be examples, so the risk is exposure through operator misuse, not confirmed exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Cluster Commands In Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The static command-execution findings are Markdown examples for kubectl, helm, psql, and alembic, not code executed automatically by the skill. They still require care because they create, modify, or delete Kubernetes and database resources.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present in fenced Markdown blocks and are consistent with this DevOps skill. They are not hidden execution paths, but they can affect production infrastructure when run.

content.value_statement: Running PostgreSQL on Kubernetes requires careful operator, storage, secret, and backup configuration. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a CloudNativePG deployment workflow for development and production clusters.
actual_capabilities: Installs CloudNativePG with direct manifests or Helm chart commands.
Provides development and production PostgreSQL cluster manifests.
Shows how CloudNativePG connection secrets are consumed by applications.
Documents service endpoints for read-write and read-only database traffic.
Covers S3 backup configuration and scheduled backup resources.
Includes monitoring, troubleshooting, and cleanup commands for clusters.
limitations: Does not deploy managed databases such as RDS, Cloud SQL, Neon, or Supabase.
Does not validate storage classes, node capacity, or cloud permissions automatically.
Uses example credentials and bucket names that must be replaced before use.
Does not replace a production disaster recovery or compliance review.

use_cases.title: Create a local database cluster
use_cases.description: Use the development manifest to run a small CloudNativePG cluster for feature testing.

use_cases.title: Prepare a production PostgreSQL rollout
use_cases.description: Use the high availability manifest, resource settings, monitoring, and backup sections as a deployment checklist.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot database operations
use_cases.description: Use the status, logs, connectivity, and migration examples to diagnose a Kubernetes PostgreSQL deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Start a development cluster
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to help me deploy a single-instance CloudNativePG PostgreSQL cluster for local development. Include the commands I should review before running.

prompt_templates.title: Plan production high availability
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a three-instance PostgreSQL cluster on Kubernetes with storage, resource requests, pod anti-affinity, and monitoring enabled.

prompt_templates.title: Add backups and secrets
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add S3 backups, scheduled backups, and application DATABASE_URL secret references to my CloudNativePG deployment plan.

prompt_templates.title: Review an existing manifest
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my CloudNativePG manifest for high availability, backup coverage, connection secret handling, and risky cleanup steps.

output_examples.input: Help me deploy PostgreSQL for a staging namespace.
output: A short operator installation plan using CloudNativePG.
A staging cluster configuration with storage and resource guidance.
A list of secret, service, and readiness checks to run after deployment.

output_examples.input: Review my production database setup for missing operations work.
output: A gap list covering backups, monitoring, service endpoints, and credential handling.
Recommended CloudNativePG settings for high availability and maintenance.
Follow-up checks for storage classes, S3 credentials, and restore testing.

output_examples.input: My app cannot connect to pg-cluster-rw.
output: A troubleshooting sequence for pod status, services, secrets, and DNS names.
A safe connectivity test from inside the cluster.
Likely causes such as wrong service name, namespace, or credential mismatch.

best_practices: Pin and review operator manifests before applying them to shared clusters.
Use Kubernetes secrets or external secret managers instead of inline database passwords.
Test backups and restores before relying on the cluster for production data.
anti_patterns: Do not paste example passwords or bucket names into production manifests.
Do not delete persistent volume claims unless data loss is intended and approved.
Do not expose decoded connection strings in chat logs, tickets, terminals, or shell history.

faq.question: Does this skill create a managed PostgreSQL service?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on PostgreSQL running inside Kubernetes with CloudNativePG.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include production high availability guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a three-instance cluster pattern, resource settings, anti-affinity, and monitoring.

faq.question: Does it handle database backups?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows CloudNativePG backup settings for S3-compatible object storage and scheduled backups.

faq.question: Are the included passwords safe to use?
faq.answer: No. They are placeholders and must be replaced with secrets generated for your environment.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it includes remote manifests, secret handling, and cluster deletion commands.
